mnemonics.title: File Attributes
mnemonics.content: NAME TYPE LOCations SIZE PROtection TIME: N-T-L-S-P-T represents the seven essential file attributes: Name, Type, Location, Size, Protection, and Time-related attributes (created, modified, accessed).

mnemonics.title: Basic File Operations
mnemonics.content: CR-W-R-S-D-T: CREATE-WRITE-READ-SEEK-DELETE-TRUNCATE. Remember as 'CReaTe WRiTe Read SeeK DeleTe' - these are the fundamental operations you can perform on files.

mnemonics.title: File Access Methods
mnemonics.content: SADI: Sequential, Access, Direct, Indexed - four main access methods. Think 'SAD' because random access can be confusing at first!

acronyms.expansion: Formatted Information Logic Element - A logical unit of data storage
acronyms.usage: Used to organize and store persistent data on secondary storage devices

acronyms.expansion: Operation to Prepare and Establish iNformation eXchange
acronyms.usage: System call to prepare a file for read/write access, returning a file descriptor

acronyms.expansion: System call to Enable External Knowledge
acronyms.usage: Operation to move the file position pointer to a specific location for random access
Marker indicating no more data to read in a file during sequential access

visualTips.visualization: Think of a cassette tape or DVD - you must go through songs in order. To reach track 10, you must pass through tracks 1-9.
Think of a DVD with chapter selection or a book's table of contents - you can jump directly to any location without reading what's before it.
Imagine a funnel: data flows through a buffer (funnel) into the file. The buffer collects small amounts and releases them together, reducing the number of disk writes.
Think of your eyes scanning a document. The position pointer is where your eyes are currently focused - that's where reading will start, and it moves as you read.

analogies.analogy: Like a physical file folder in an office cabinet - it holds related documents together, has a label (filename), and can be found in a specific drawer (directory).
Like the table of contents in a book or a filing cabinet's index cards - it tells you where different files are located without containing the actual file content.
Like a library card or checkout number - you don't get the book itself, just a reference number that the librarian uses to find and manage your request.
Like a delivery truck collecting packages from multiple houses before going to the post office - fewer trips (I/O operations) but delivers more data at once.
